name: Market risk
description: Loss from moving prices: measures, methods and sensitivities.

glossary.VaR: Value at Risk — a quantile-based measure of the loss threshold not expected to be exceeded over a given horizon at a stated confidence
glossary.ES: Expected Shortfall — a coherent risk measure averaging losses beyond the VaR threshold, adopted by Basel FRTB at 97.5%
glossary.CVaR: Conditional Value at Risk — another name for Expected Shortfall
glossary.FRTB: Fundamental Review of the Trading Book — the Basel Committee's redesign of market-risk capital rules replacing VaR with ES
glossary.sVaR: Stressed VaR — VaR calibrated to a continuous 12-month period of significant financial stress rather than recent calm data
glossary.EWMA: Exponentially Weighted Moving Average — a volatility estimator giving more weight to recent observations via a decay factor
glossary.GARCH: Generalised Autoregressive Conditional Heteroskedasticity — a time-series model for time-varying conditional variance
glossary.DV01: Dollar Value of 01 — the change in a position's value for a one-basis-point move in interest rates
glossary.PV01: Present Value of 01 — equivalent to DV01 for fixed-income positions
glossary.PCA: Principal Component Analysis — a technique decomposing correlated risk factors into orthogonal components ranked by variance explained

primer:
**Market risk** is loss from moving prices and rates (rates, FX, equity, commodities, credit spreads) — the dominant risk of a *trading book*.

**Value at Risk (VaR)** is the loss not exceeded over a horizon at confidence $\\alpha$ — the $\\alpha$-quantile of losses: $\\mathrm{VaR}_\\alpha(L)=\\inf\\{l:P(L>l)\\le 1-\\alpha\\}$. It says nothing about *how bad* losses beyond it are, and is **not subadditive** in general (except for elliptical/normal distributions), so it is **not coherent**. Always quote both confidence and horizon (regulatory VaR traditionally 99% over **10** days). **Expected Shortfall (ES/CVaR)** is the average loss in the worst $(1-\\alpha)$ tail *beyond* VaR: $\\mathrm{ES}_\\alpha=\\frac{1}{1-\\alpha}\\int_\\alpha^1\\mathrm{VaR}_u\\,du$. It is coherent, always $\\ge\\mathrm{VaR}$, and Basel's **FRTB** adopted it at **97.5%** to replace 99% VaR.

**Coherence** (Artzner 1999): monotonicity, translation invariance, positive homogeneity, **subadditivity** $\\rho(X+Y)\\le\\rho(X)+\\rho(Y)$ — diversification must never be penalised. **Spectral** measures average quantiles weighted by a risk-aversion function; ES is the uniform-weight case.

**VaR methods.** *Parametric (variance–covariance)*: assume normal returns, $\\mathrm{VaR}_\\alpha=z_\\alpha\\sigma_P\\sqrt{h}$ — fast but understates fat tails. *Historical simulation*: re-price under actual past moves, take the empirical quantile — **no distributional assumption** (suffers ghost effects). *Monte Carlo*: simulate scenarios with full revaluation — flexible for optionality, costly. *Square-root-of-time* scaling $\\mathrm{VaR}(h)=\\mathrm{VaR}(1)\\sqrt{h}$ holds only for **i.i.d. returns with no autocorrelation**. *Stressed VaR (sVaR)* is calibrated to a continuous **12-month** stress period (Basel 2.5; capital $\\propto\\mathrm{VaR}+\\mathrm{sVaR}$).

**Backtesting** counts *exceptions* (loss > VaR); expected exceptions $=(1-\\alpha)\\times$ days. The *Kupiec POF* test (LR, $\\chi^2_1$) checks unconditional coverage only; the *Christoffersen* test adds **independence** (catching clustered breaches). Basel's traffic-light test maps the 250-day count to multiplier zones.

**Volatility** is the std of returns ($\\sigma_{\\text{ann}}=\\sigma_{\\text{daily}}\\sqrt{252}$). *Clustering* (large follows large) violates i.i.d. and motivates EWMA/GARCH. *Implied* vol (from option prices) typically **exceeds realised** vol on average (variance risk premium).

**Sensitivities.** *Delta–gamma*: $\\Delta V\\approx\\delta\\,\\Delta S+\\tfrac12\\gamma(\\Delta S)^2$. *The Greeks*: delta (spot), gamma (delta), vega (vol), theta (time), rho (rates). *DV01/PV01*: value change per 1bp of rates. *Convexity*: bond price rises more than it falls for equal yield moves, $\\frac{\\Delta P}{P}\\approx -D\\Delta y+\\tfrac12 C(\\Delta y)^2$. *Basis risk*: hedge and exposure don't move in lockstep. *Beta* $=\\frac{\\mathrm{Cov}(r_i,r_m)}{\\mathrm{Var}(r_m)}$ is systematic risk. *Tracking error* $=\\sqrt{\\mathrm{Var}(r_p-r_b)}$ is active risk.

**Risk attribution.** *Marginal VaR* $=\\partial\\mathrm{VaR}/\\partial w_i$; *Component VaR* $=w_i\\,\\partial\\mathrm{VaR}/\\partial w_i$ sums exactly to total VaR (**Euler** allocation); *Incremental VaR* is the discrete change from adding/removing a whole trade. **FRTB liquidity horizons** range **10–120** days by risk-factor class, replacing the uniform 10-day assumption.

cards.definition: The risk of loss from movements in market prices and rates — interest rates, FX, equity, commodities and credit spreads. It is the dominant risk of a trading book and the original target of the Basel internal-models regime.

The loss threshold a portfolio will not exceed over a fixed horizon at a given confidence level — the $\alpha$-quantile of the loss distribution. It compresses market risk into a single currency figure but says nothing about how bad losses beyond it are.
cards.note: Not subadditive in general, so it is not a coherent risk measure — diversification can appear to increase it.

cards.term: Basel's [[FRTB]] replaced 99% [[VaR]] with expected shortfall measured at the {{97.5}}% confidence level.
cards.definition: The average loss conditional on being in the worst $(1-\alpha)$ tail beyond [[VaR]] — it answers 'if things go bad, how bad on average?'. It is coherent and is the measure Basel's [[FRTB]] adopted (at 97.5%) to replace 99% [[VaR]].
cards.note: Always $\mathrm{ES}_\alpha \ge \mathrm{VaR}_\alpha$; ES captures tail shape, VaR ignores it.

cards.definition: A measure $\rho$ satisfying four axioms (Artzner et al., 1999): monotonicity, translation invariance, positive homogeneity and subadditivity. Coherence formalises the intuition that diversification should never be penalised.
cards.note: [[ES]] is coherent; [[VaR]] fails subadditivity except under elliptical (e.g. normal) distributions.

cards.term: [[VaR]] is always subadditive — the risk of a merged portfolio can never exceed the sum of the two books' individual [[VaR]] figures.
cards.definition: False. [[VaR]] can violate subadditivity, so a merged book may show more [[VaR]] than the two books separately; [[ES]] (expected shortfall) is subadditive and therefore coherent. The axiom that the risk of a combined portfolio cannot exceed the sum of the parts' risks, encoding the benefit of diversification. Its violation by [[VaR]] can make a merged book look riskier than two separate ones.
cards.summary: False — [[VaR]] is not subadditive in general, which is exactly why it fails to be a coherent risk measure.
cards.note: [1] Artzner, Delbaen, Eber, Heath (1999) 'Coherent Measures of Risk', Mathematical Finance 9(3). [2] VaR (Wikipedia) — https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Value_at_risk

cards.definition: [[VaR]] computed by assuming returns are jointly normal (or elliptical) and reading the quantile off portfolio volatility. Fast and analytic, but it systematically understates losses when real returns are fat-tailed. [[EWMA]] or [[GARCH]] estimates are often used for the volatility input; [[FRTB]] replaced this as the regulatory benchmark.

cards.term: Historical-simulation [[VaR]] makes no parametric assumption about the return distribution.
cards.definition: True. [[VaR]] obtained by re-pricing today's portfolio under each of the actual market moves observed in a look-back window, then taking the empirical quantile. It makes no distributional assumption but is only as rich as the sample period it draws on. Unlike parametric [[VaR]], it can naturally accommodate fat tails; [[ES]] can also be estimated from the same scenarios.
cards.note: Ghost effects: a big move stays in the window then suddenly drops out, jumping the estimate.
cards.summary: True — historical simulation re-prices under actual past moves and takes the empirical quantile, assuming no distributional form.

cards.definition: [[VaR]] from many simulated market scenarios drawn from a chosen stochastic model, with full revaluation of nonlinear positions. The most flexible method for optionality and path dependence, at high computational cost.
cards.formula: draw $\mathbf{r}^{(1)},\dots,\mathbf{r}^{(N)}$, revalue, take the empirical $\alpha$-quantile of losses

cards.term: Regulatory market-risk capital traditionally measured [[VaR]] at 99% confidence over a {{10}}-day holding period.
cards.definition: [[VaR]] is defined only relative to a confidence level (e.g. 99%) and a holding period (e.g. 1 or 10 days). Higher confidence and longer horizons both raise the figure; regulatory market-risk capital traditionally used 99% over 10 days. [[FRTB]] introduced [[sVaR]] alongside plain [[VaR]] to address procyclicality.
cards.note: Always quote both — '€1M VaR' is meaningless without them.

cards.term: Scaling [[VaR]] from one horizon to another by the {{square root of time}} is valid only when returns are i.i.d. with no autocorrelation.
cards.definition: A convention that scales volatility and [[VaR]] from one horizon to another by the square root of time, valid only when returns are i.i.d. with zero autocorrelation. It breaks down under volatility clustering or mean reversion.

Validating a [[VaR]] model by comparing realised P&L against the predicted quantile and counting exceptions (days the loss exceeded [[VaR]]). The exception rate should match the model's tail probability over time. [[ES]] is harder to backtest directly because it depends on the full tail, not just the breach count.

A day on which the actual loss exceeds the day's [[VaR]] estimate. Too many exceptions signals an understated model; clustered exceptions signal it fails to react to changing volatility. Under [[FRTB]] the traffic-light framework was updated, with [[ES]] replacing [[VaR]] as the primary capital metric.
cards.note: Basel's traffic-light test maps the count over 250 days to green/amber/red multiplier zones.

cards.definition: A likelihood-ratio test of whether the observed number of [[VaR]] exceptions is consistent with the model's stated coverage — testing unconditional coverage only. It has low power with the short samples typically available.

An extension of Kupiec that jointly tests correct coverage and independence of [[VaR]] exceptions, catching models whose breaches cluster in time. Independence matters because clustered losses are what threaten solvency. Useful complement to [[ES]]-based backtests.
cards.note: Combines unconditional coverage with a test that today's exception doesn't predict tomorrow's.

cards.term: [[sVaR]] is calibrated to a continuous {{12-month}} period of significant financial stress rather than recent data.
cards.definition: [[VaR]] calibrated to a continuous 12-month period of significant financial stress (e.g. 2008) rather than recent calm data. Basel 2.5 added [[sVaR]] to total market-risk capital to counter the procyclicality of plain [[VaR]]. [[FRTB]] subsequently replaced the VaR + sVaR framework with [[ES]].

The standard deviation of returns — the primary scale of market risk. It is unobservable and must be estimated; annualisation assumes independence across periods. Common estimators include [[EWMA]] and [[GARCH]], both of which feed directly into parametric [[VaR]].

The empirical regularity that large moves tend to follow large moves and calm follows calm, so volatility is persistent and predictable in magnitude. It motivates time-varying models ([[EWMA]], [[GARCH]]) over constant-variance assumptions.
cards.note: A direct violation of the i.i.d. assumption behind simple [[VaR]] scaling.

cards.term: Implied volatility typically exceeds realised volatility on average.
cards.definition: True. Realised (historical) volatility is measured from past returns; implied volatility is backed out of option prices and reflects the market's forward expectation plus a variance risk premium. Implied typically exceeds realised on average. The gap is a traded risk factor and affects [[VaR]] model choice.
cards.note: Their gap, and the implied-vol surface's skew, are themselves traded risk factors.
cards.summary: True — implied vol embeds a variance risk premium, so it tends to sit above subsequently realised vol.

cards.definition: A second-order Taylor expansion approximating an option's value change from a move in the underlying, capturing curvature that a linear (delta-only) approximation misses. It underlies fast [[VaR]] for option books.

Sensitivities of a derivative's value to its risk factors, the language of options risk: delta to spot, gamma to delta, vega to volatility, theta to time decay, and rho to rates. Aggregating Greeks across a book is the first step in parametric [[VaR]].
cards.formula: $\delta=\frac{\partial V}{\partial S},\;\gamma=\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial S^2},\;\nu=\frac{\partial V}{\partial \sigma},\;\Theta=\frac{\partial V}{\partial t}$

cards.definition: The change in a position's value for a one-basis-point move in interest rates — the workhorse measure of rate sensitivity for fixed-income desks. Aggregated across the curve it gives the book's rate exposure by tenor. It feeds directly into interest-rate [[VaR]] calculations.

The second-order sensitivity of a bond's price to yield: price rises more when yields fall than it drops when yields rise by the same amount. Positive convexity is valuable to a holder and must be hedged in delta-neutral books. A [[DV01]]-hedged position may still carry convexity risk, which shows up in [[VaR]] under large yield moves.

The residual risk that a hedge and the exposure it hedges do not move in lockstep, because they reference different but related underlyings, maturities or locations. It is what remains after an imperfect hedge.
cards.note: Example: hedging jet fuel with crude futures leaves crack-spread basis risk.

cards.definition: The sensitivity of an asset's return to the return of the market portfolio — its systematic, undiversifiable risk under the CAPM. Beta above one amplifies market moves; below one dampens them. It enters equity [[VaR]] directly; [[GARCH]]-based beta estimates allow for time-varying systematic risk.
The volatility of the difference between a portfolio's return and its benchmark — the measure of active risk for an index-relative mandate. Low tracking error means the portfolio hugs its benchmark.
The change in portfolio [[VaR]] per unit increase in a position — the partial derivative of [[VaR]] with respect to that holding. It identifies which positions add or hedge risk at the margin.

cards.term: Component [[VaR]]s sum exactly to total portfolio [[VaR]] under the {{Euler}} allocation.
cards.definition: The portion of total portfolio [[VaR]] attributable to a position, accounting for its diversification interaction with the rest of the book. Component [[VaR]]s sum exactly to total [[VaR]] (Euler allocation).

The change in total portfolio [[VaR]] from adding or removing an entire position or trade — what a new trade actually costs in risk terms. Unlike marginal [[VaR]] it is a discrete, not infinitesimal, difference.

A coherent family that averages quantiles of the loss distribution weighted by a non-decreasing risk-aversion function, putting more weight on worse outcomes. [[ES]] is the special case with uniform weight over the tail; [[VaR]] is not a spectral measure because it assigns all weight to a single quantile.

cards.term: [[FRTB]] liquidity horizons range from 10 to {{120}} days depending on the risk-factor class.
cards.definition: In [[FRTB]], the assumed time to exit or hedge a risk position in stressed conditions without materially affecting prices — ranging from 10 to 120 days by risk-factor class. Less liquid factors get longer horizons and thus higher capital. The framework uses [[ES]] rather than [[VaR]] as the underlying risk measure, scaled to each liquidity horizon.
cards.note: Replaces the uniform 10-day [[VaR]] assumption with risk-factor-specific holding periods.

cards.term: On this loss/return distribution, which measure is the average loss in the shaded tail beyond the cutoff?
cards.definition: [[ES]] (Expected Shortfall, a.k.a. [[CVaR]]) — the mean loss beyond the [[VaR]] threshold (the shaded tail). [[VaR]] is only the cutoff (the dashed line); [[ES]] averages everything past it, so [[ES]] is always at least as large as [[VaR]] and captures tail severity [[VaR]] ignores.
cards.summary: Expected Shortfall ([[ES]]/[[CVaR]]) — the average loss in the tail past the [[VaR]] cutoff.
